verses.text: Gósen (veja nota de estudo em 45.10 ) não é mencionado em textos egípcios antigos; o nome que tinha em escritos egípcios posteriores era “a região de Ramsés” ( **47.11** ; veja **Êx 1.11** ). Era fértil e perto de José na corte, o que sugere que estava no lado leste do delta do Nilo.
O Faraó respondeu como José esperava, dando à família de Jacó a melhor parte da terra; ele até deu a alguns dos irmãos a supervisão de seu próprio gado ( **47.6** ).\n\n

Quando Jacó entrou na corte de Faraó e quando saiu, ele abençoou Faraó, uma indicação da posição de Jacó como representante de Deus (veja **Hb 7.7** ). Deus havia prometido que abençoaria aqueles que abençoassem a família de Abraão ( **Gn 12.2-3** ), então ele agora abençoou Faraó e o Egito.

47:11 Joseph placed his father and his brethren . . . in the best of the land—best pasture land in lower Egypt. Goshen, "the land of verdure," lay along the Pelusiac or eastern branch of the Nile. It included a part of the district of Heliopolis, or "On," the capital, and on the east stretched out a considerable length into the desert. The ground included within these boundaries was a rich and fertile extent of natural meadow, and admirably adapted for the purposes of the Hebrew shepherds (compare Gen 49:24;

47:12 And there was no bread in all the land,.... The land of Egypt and the parts adjacent, but in Pharaoh's storehouses, all being consumed that were in private hands the first two years of the famine: for the famine was very sore; severe, pressed very hard: so that the land of Egypt, and all the land of Canaan, fainted by reason of the famine; that is, the inhabitants of both countries, their spirits sunk, as well as their flesh failed for want of food: or "raged" (b);

O Senhor abençoou Faraó porque Faraó estava abençoando os descendentes de Abraão ( **12.3** ). Através da administração sábia de José no Egito, o Senhor salvou o povo da fome e prosperou Faraó. Ao vender comida aos egípcios durante os anos de fome, José aceitou dinheiro, gado e, por fim, terras como pagamento, até que quase todo o Egito pertencesse a Faraó. Enquanto isso, Deus providenciou a Israel algumas das melhores terras no Egito onde eles poderiam viver, trabalhar e multiplicar.\n\n

ele fez de todos eles escravos: Em escrita hebraica, a diferença é muito sutil entre escravos (hebraico ‘abadim) e cidades (hebraico ‘arim). Mover as pessoas para as cidades não se encaixa muito bem no contexto, então a maioria das traduções escolhe escravos como a leitura que faz mais sentido (cp. **47.20** ).

47:22 Only the land of the priests bought he not—These lands were inalienable, being endowments by which the temples were supported. The priests for themselves received an annual allowance of provision from the state, and it would evidently have been the height of cruelty to withhold that allowance when their lands were incapable of being tilled.

47:23 Joseph said, Behold, &c.—The lands being sold to the government (Gen 47:19-20), seed would be distributed for the first crop after the famine; and the people would occupy them as tenants-at-will on the payment of a produce rent, almost the same rule as obtains in Egypt in the present day.

47:24 And they said, thou hast saved our lives,.... Preserved them from death through famine, by laying up stores of corn, which he had sold out to them for their money, cattle, and land, or otherwise they must have perished, they and theirs, and this favour they thankfully acknowledge: let us find grace in the sight of my lord, and we will be Pharaoh's servants; signifying, that they esteemed it a great favour to be so on the foot of the bargain made with them, and they desired a continuance in it.

47:25 And Joseph made it a law over the land of Egypt unto this day,.... With the consent of Pharaoh, his nobles, and all the people of the land, who readily came into it; and so it became, a fundamental law of their constitution, and which continued to the times of Moses, the writer of this history: that Pharaoh should have the fifth part; that is, of the increase the whole land of Egypt produced: except the land of the priests only, which became not Pharaoh's;

47:26 And Joseph made it a law - That the people should hold their land from the king, and give him the fifth part of the produce as a yearly tax. Beyond this it appears the king had no farther demands. The whole of this conduct of Joseph has been as strongly censured by some as applauded by others. It is natural for men to run into extremes in attacking or defending any position. Sober and judicious men will consider what Joseph did by Divine appointment as a prophet of God, and what he did merely as a statesman from the circumstances of the case, the complexion of the times, and the character of the people over whom he presided.

Deus abençoou seu povo de acordo com sua promessa a Abraão de que seus descendentes seriam inumeráveis ( **15.5** ; **22.17** ). Eles tiveram que esperar pelo cumprimento da segunda promessa, de que possuiriam a terra de Canaã ( **17.8** ).

47:28 And the time drew nigh that Israel must die,.... As all men must, by the appointment of God, even good men, the Israel of God; though they shall not die a spiritual death, nor an eternal one, yet a corporeal one, which is for their good, and is a blessing to them; the sting being removed, and so not a penal evil, which is owing to Christ's dying for them, who has abolished death as such; and there is a time fixed for their death, beyond which they must not live, and before which they must not die, but when the time comes there is no avoiding it;

enterre-me com meus antepassados: Cp. **49.29-33** . Jacó queria ser enterrado com Abraão e Isaque na caverna de Macpela (veja nota de estudo em 49.29-33 ; veja **23.1-20** ; **25.7-10** ; **35.27-29** ). • Coloque sua mão sob minha coxa: Este costume (cp. **24.1-9** e nota de estudo em 24.2 ) era um juramento sério para continuar a aliança, que tinha os descendentes inumeráveis na Terra Prometida como principal promessa.

enterre-me com meus antepassados: Cp. **49.29-33** . Jacó queria ser enterrado com Abraão e Isaque na caverna de Macpela (veja nota de estudo em 49.29-33 ; veja **23.1-20** ; **25.7-10** ; **35.27-29** ). • Coloque sua mão sob minha coxa: Este costume (cp. **24.1-9** e nota de estudo em 24.2 ) era um juramento sério para continuar a aliança, que tinha os descendentes inumeráveis na Terra Prometida como principal promessa.
Quando o juramento foi feito, Jacó se curvou humildemente em adoração e agradeceu ao Senhor por garantir que ele seria enterrado com seus ancestrais na terra da promessa (cp. **1Rs 1.47** ).
